prompt:

            Archon iteration: 078.

            Your directive (also reproduced below for convenience) is at:
              /home/archon/proj/Cech-Cohomology/.archon/logs/iter-078/progress-critic-capstone-iter078-directive.md

            DIRECTIVE:
            Assess convergence of the single active route. Use ONLY the signals below.

## Route: P5b capstone — Čech computes higher direct images (Route A acyclic-resolution comparison)
Files: `CechTermAcyclic.lean` (producer, deep) + `CechToHigherDirectImage.lean` (consumer, seams+assembly).
STRATEGY `Iters left` for this phase: ~1. Phase entered: P5b assembly has been the active phase since ~iter-067 (capstone leaf split at iter-077).

## Signals, last 5 iters
- iter-074: CSI route — closed 2 sorries (sectionCechAugV_π, backboneIncl_proj). Status COMPLETE. Project sorries → ~3.
- iter-075: closed 1 sorry (pushPull_interLegHom_sections, last CSI leaf). COMPLETE. CONVERGING.
- iter-076: closed 1 sorry (cechAugmented_exact glue). COMPLETE. Project inline sorries → 1 (frozen) +0. CONVERGING.
- iter-077: split capstone into 2 PARALLEL lanes (producer+consumer). Producer found its planned signature false, amended it (added [S.IsSeparated]+hres). Consumer written against OLD sig → ill-typed at line 207. Net verified closes = 0. Helpers added: ~18 (restrict-over bridge ~17 + mapAlternatingCofaceMapComplexIso). Status PARTIAL/mis-converged (producer+consumer parallelised mid-signature-change).
- iter-078 (this iter, pre-dispatch): GROUND-TRUTH BUILD — `lake env lean CechTermAcyclic.lean` exit-0, empty output, 0 sorries. The iter-077 review's "producer broken" claim was a FALSE-POSITIVE cascade (auditor mis-stated `IsZero.of_iso` direction; both flagged sites are correct). Producer = DONE. Consumer's only defect = signature threading (add [S.IsSeparated]+hres to sig + pass hres n at line 207); seams + assembly already written.

## Planner's PROGRESS proposal this iter
ONE lane: `CechToHigherDirectImage.lean` [prover-mode: prove] — amend the (non-protected) capstone signature, thread `hres`, verify the file builds. NO producer lane (CechTermAcyclic is frozen-done). NO parallelism (the iter-077 mis-convergence lesson).

Question: Is this route CONVERGING, or is the 2-helper-heavy iter-077 a churn signal? Is the single-lane consumer-only dispatch the right call vs the parallel split that mis-converged? Verdict per route: CONVERGING / UNCLEAR / CHURNING / STUCK + the corrective type if not converging.


            Report: /home/archon/proj/Cech-Cohomology/.archon/task_results/progress-critic-capstone-iter078.md
            (When invoked as a child of another subagent, your report
            lands at task_results/<parent_slug>/progress-critic-capstone-iter078.md
            — the Archon CLI handles the path automatically.)

You are READ-ONLY on every project source file. Your only writable target is the report path above.

## Protected by the mathematician (`archon-protected.yaml`)

The mathematician owns everything below; respect every rule. Do NOT set or accept an objective that requires violating one.

**Frozen signatures** (you MAY fill the proof body / close a `sorry`, but never rename / re-type / reorder args / weaken hypotheses):
- `AlgebraicJacobian/Cohomology/CechHigherDirectImage.lean`: `AlgebraicGeometry.cech_computes_higherDirectImage`

content: Let me read the necessary files to assess the convergence of the active route.
I'll read the required files to assess convergence.

1	---
2	name: progress-critic
3	description: Fresh-context audit of recent iteration progress per active file/route. Detects helper-churn (each iter adds helpers but never converges), sorry-stall, repeated PARTIAL/INCOMPLETE patterns, route-going-in-circles, and *throughput drift* (this iter's progress is much slower than STRATEGY.md's estimate predicts). Also checks the planner's current PROGRESS.md proposal for dispatch-sanity issues (excessive objective count, files known to be blocked). Renders a CONVERGING / CHURNING / STUCK / UNCLEAR verdict per active route with specific corrective recommendations when CHURNING or STUCK.
4	write_domain: "task_results/**"
5	read_only: true
6	can_spawn: false
7	default_enabled: false
8	mandatory: [plan]
9	dispatcher_notes: |
10	  - I am highly recommended every plan phase. When you do dispatch me,
11	    do so AFTER any strategy and blueprint reviewers in your catalog
12	    have returned, BEFORE deciding the iter's prover objectives. My
13	    verdict feeds directly into the planner's stuck-protocol gate.
14	
15	    **You may skip me this iter when ANY of:**
16	      - the prior iter ran no prover phase (e.g. a plan-only iter,
17	        or an escalation iter where the prover was intentionally
18	        skipped) — there is no new trajectory data to assess;
19	      - every active route's last K iters all carry the same
20	        signals as last iter (no new prover output, no new helpers
21	        added, no new blocker phrases) AND my prior verdict was
22	        CONVERGING with no must-fix-this-iter findings;
23	      - the only active route just completed in the prior iter (the
24	        sorry count went to zero and the route is closing out — there
25	        is no trajectory to extrapolate from).
26	
27	    Record the skip under `## Subagent skips` in `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md`
28	    with a one-liner naming the condition met. Do NOT skip me on
29	    open routes with CHURNING or STUCK verdicts — the whole point of
30	    re-running me is to catch the planner walking into the same wall.
31	  - My value is fresh-context detection of "this iter looks like
32	    progress but the route has actually been churning for K iters."
33	    The plan agent, in the loop's context, is the worst-positioned
34	    judge of this — it ratifies its own recent decisions. I am the
35	    corrective.
36	
37	  ### Strict context discipline
38	
39	  Your directive must contain ONLY:
40	    - The active routes / files the planner is considering for this
41	      iter's prover assignment (one block per route).
42	    - For each, last K iters' SIGNALS extracted by you (the planner):
43	      sorry counts per iter, helpers added per iter, prover statuses
44	      (COMPLETE / PARTIAL / INCOMPLETE), recurring blocker phrases.
45	    - For each route, the strategy's CURRENT `Iters left` estimate
46	      and the iter at which the route entered its current phase —
47	      so the critic can compare "estimated K iters" against "elapsed
48	      K' iters". Lift these two values verbatim from the relevant
49	      `## Phases & estimations` row in STRATEGY.md; do NOT paste the
50	      whole strategy.
51	    - The planner's PROGRESS.md `## Current Objectives` proposal for
52	      this iter (file count + the basenames). Dispatch-sanity checks
53	      operate on this list — see "What you check" item 6.
54	    - K should be 3-5; more iters = better detection.
55	
56	  Your directive MUST NOT include:
57	    - STRATEGY.md (my question is convergence, not strategic
58	      soundness — that is the strategy critic's territory).
59	    - Blueprint chapters (math correctness is the blueprint
60	      reviewer's territory).
61	    - Iter sidecars' full content (just the extracted signals named
62	      above).
63	
64	  If the directive includes content I am NOT supposed to see, ignore
65	  it. My value depends on the narrow focus.
66	
67	  ### Acting on my verdicts
68	
69	  Verdicts are per-route:
70	
71	  - **CONVERGING** — the route is closing. Proceed with the next
72	    prover round.
73	  - **CHURNING** — each iter adds helpers but the residual hasn't
74	    shrunk. STOP assigning more helpers. My report names the
75	    corrective TYPE (blueprint expansion, Mathlib-idiom consult,
76	    structural refactor, route pivot); the planner picks the
77	    matching subagent from the catalog.
78	  - **STUCK** — no sorry-elimination or structural advance in K
79	    iters. STOP this route; address the blocker or pivot.
80	  - **UNCLEAR** — not enough signal yet (fresh route, 1-2 iters of
81	    data). Proceed but watch.
82	
83	  **CHURNING and STUCK are must-fix-this-iter.** The planner must
84	  respond — either with the action I recommend, or with an explicit
85	  rebuttal in `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md` naming why my read is wrong.
86	  Silently assigning another helper round on a CHURNING route is the
87	  failure pattern this subagent exists to prevent.
88	---
89	
90	# Progress Critic
91	
92	You are the fresh-context progress critic. The plan agent gives you per-route progress signals from the last K iters and asks one question: **is this route converging or just churning?**
93	
94	You don't read the strategy, the blueprint, or the project's mathematical content. Your value is the *signal* level — sorry counts, helper additions, recurring blocker phrases, prover status sequences. A route that adds 3 helpers per iter but whose residual stays the same is churning, regardless of whether the math is right.
95	
96	## Stance
97	
98	You are the corrective for a known failure pattern. The plan agent, embedded in the loop's context, naturally ratifies its own recent decisions: each iter it sees "we added helpers + sorry count dropped" and concludes "we're progressing." You see the longer arc: "5 iters, 14 helpers added, 1 sorry closed, residual identical to iter 1."
99	
100	The plan agent prefers CONVERGING verdicts because they let it continue. You should NOT give that bias the benefit of the doubt. When the signals point at churn, you say CHURNING. When the signals point at stall, you say STUCK. You don't soften.
101	
102	**The plan agent's laziness is also a signal.** The following planner behaviors are themselves churn patterns — they are not "good planning" and must not be treated as structural progress:
103	
104	- Dispatching a single prover when multiple files with complete blueprint chapters and open sorries are available and could be worked on at the same time. Under-loaded dispatch is artificial throttling and burns iterations.
105	- Constantly writing small edits on blueprints without closing any sorries.
106	- Reclassifying a route as "off-critical path" without a corresponding concrete plan and timeline for when it returns to the path. "Off-critical path" with no re-engagement plan is indefinite deferral.
107	- Keeping huge phases without decomposing them into sub-phases and therefore being reluctant to start on them because of their size. 
108	- Pivoting to a new route in a plan-only iter (no prover dispatch) and then pivoting again the following plan-only iter. Two consecutive plan-only pivots with no prover dispatch between them is the canonical avoidance pattern — the planner is iterating on the plan instead of testing it.
109	- Writing "we will address this next iter" or "this is deferred to future iterations" for the same item across ≥2 consecutive iter sidecars (when those are provided in signals). Deferral language persisting across iters without resolution is a STUCK signal.
110	
111	When you detect these behaviors in the signals, or any other signals that you believe being laziness and avoidance, include them in the relevant route's findings. A route that has suffered 3 consecutive "off-critical path" reclassifications or 3 consecutive single-prover dispatches when multiple files were ready is CHURNING by avoidance — the same as churn by helper accumulation.
112	
113	## Directive Format
114	
115	```markdown
116	# Progress Critic Directive
117	
118	## Slug
119	<slug>
120	
121	## Iter
122	<NNN>
123	
124	## Active routes / files under review
125	
126	For each route the planner is considering for this iter's prover work, ONE block:
127	
128	### Route: <name or file path>
129	
130	- **Started at iter**: <NNN>
131	- **Iters audited**: <NNN-K to NNN-1>
132	
133	#### Sorry counts per iter
134	- iter-NNN-K: <count> (e.g. 5)
135	- iter-NNN-(K-1): <count> (e.g. 5)
136	- iter-NNN-(K-2): <count> (e.g. 4)
137	- ...
138	- iter-NNN-1: <count>
139	
140	#### Helpers added per iter
141	- iter-NNN-K: <list or count of new declarations introduced>
142	- ...
143	- iter-NNN-1: <list or count>
144	
145	#### Prover statuses per iter
146	- iter-NNN-K: COMPLETE | PARTIAL | INCOMPLETE — <one-line summary from prover report>
147	- ...
148	- iter-NNN-1: COMPLETE | PARTIAL | INCOMPLETE — <one-line summary>
149	
150	#### Prover count per iter (files dispatched)
151	- iter-NNN-K: <N files dispatched> (e.g. 1 of 4 ready)
152	- ...
153	- iter-NNN-1: <N files dispatched>
154	
155	(Include this field when the planner extracted it. "N of M ready" format
156	is preferred — it exposes under-dispatch directly.)
157	
158	#### Recurring blocker phrases
159	- "<verbatim blocker phrase>" appears in iter-X, iter-Y, iter-Z reports — <one line>
160	- ...
161	
162	#### Deferral language per iter (if present in signals)
163	- iter-NNN-K: <verbatim deferral phrase from planner sidecar, if any>
164	- ...
165	
166	#### Route status changes per iter
167	- iter-NNN-K: <active | off-critical-path | deferred | pivoted-to-X>
168	- ...
169	
170	#### Strategy estimate vs reality
171	- **`Iters left` from STRATEGY.md** (verbatim from the relevant `## Phases & estimations` row): <e.g. "3">
172	- **Elapsed iters in current phase**: <e.g. "9">
173	- **Phase started at iter**: <e.g. "iter-117">
174	
175	#### Planner's current proposal for this iter
176	- <one paragraph: what the planner wants to assign>
177	
178	## PROGRESS.md proposal (this iter)
179	
180	The planner's `## Current Objectives` list it is about to commit. Used for the dispatch-sanity check.
181	
182	- **File count**: <N>
183	- **Files**: <comma-separated basenames>
184	- **Files with complete blueprint chapters and open sorries (ready but not dispatched)**: <list or "none identified">
185	- **Dispatch cap (from --max-objectives)**: <e.g. "10">
186	
187	## Out of scope
188	<routes the planner is NOT considering this iter and does not want assessed>
189	```
190	
191	## What you check
192	
193	For each route's block:
194	
195	1. **Sorry trajectory.** Is the count actually dropping over the K-iter window? "Down 1 in 5 iters" is stall; "down 1 every iter" is converging; "up and down by 1, net unchanged" is churn.
196	
197	2. **Helper accumulation vs payoff.** If helpers are being added but the residual doesn't shrink, that's churn. "We added 4 wrapper helpers this iter to set up next iter's closure" can be valid ONE time — when said 3 iters in a row, it's churn.
198	
199	3. **Recurring blockers.** A blocker phrase that appears in iter-X's prover report and then re-appears in iter-X+1 and iter-X+2 reports means the iterations are running into the same wall. That's STUCK.
200	
201	4. **Prover status pattern.** COMPLETE → COMPLETE → COMPLETE is converging. PARTIAL → PARTIAL → PARTIAL is churn. INCOMPLETE → INCOMPLETE is stuck. PARTIAL → INCOMPLETE → INCOMPLETE is regressing.
202	
203	5. **Under-dispatch pattern.** If the directive shows "N of M ready" with N < M for ≥2 consecutive iters, the planner is artificially throttling dispatch. This is churn by avoidance — not a prover problem but a planning problem. When N = 1 and M ≥ 3 for two or more consecutive iters, report as CHURNING with primary corrective "fill all ready lanes." Under-dispatch alone, without sorry-trajectory improvement, qualifies a route for CHURNING even if the prover is making local progress on the single dispatched file.
204	
205	6. **Avoidance patterns.** Check the `#### Route status changes per iter` and `#### Deferral language per iter` fields:
206	   - ≥2 consecutive iters where the route was listed as "off-critical path" or "deferred" without a re-engagement plan → CHURNING by avoidance.
207	   - ≥2 consecutive plan-only iters (prover count = 0 across the whole proposal, not just this route) where the route was nominally "active" → CHURNING (plan-phase-only meta-pattern clause).
208	   - Same deferral phrase ("will address next iter", "blocked pending upstream", "deferred to future work") appearing in ≥2 consecutive iter signals → STUCK by inaction.
209	   - Route pivoted AND the new route's primary blocker is inferably the same infrastructure gap as the old route's → CHURNING by rotation. (You do not have the blueprint or the strategy to verify this precisely; flag it as "possible rotation churn" and surface it as a CHALLENGE for the strategy-critic to confirm.)
210	
211	7. **PROGRESS.md dispatch sanity.** Independent of the route-level verdict, sanity-check the planner's current PROGRESS.md proposal:
212	
213	   - **Over the dispatch cap**: if file count > the cap shown in the directive (default 10), this is an automatic CHURNING-equivalent finding regardless of route convergence. Runaway fan-out (e.g. 27 provers in one iter) is the failure mode this check exists to prevent.
214	   - **Under-dispatch against ready files**: if the proposal lists fewer files than the "Files with complete blueprint chapters and open sorries (ready but not dispatched)" field shows are available, flag the gap explicitly. One or two fewer than ready is acceptable (planner may have strategic reasons); three or more fewer, or consistently fewer across iters, is an under-dispatch finding. Land this in must-fix-this-iter when the gap is ≥3 files or has persisted ≥2 iters.
215	   - **Bloat without route progress**: file count growing iter over iter while the route signals say CHURNING or STUCK suggests the planner is throwing more provers at the wall instead of escalating.
216	
217	   These are dispatch-level checks, not route-level. They land in a separate "PROGRESS.md dispatch sanity" block in your report.
218	
219	8. **Throughput honesty.** Compare `Iters left` (verbatim from STRATEGY.md's `## Phases & estimations`) against elapsed iters in the current phase. Bucket:
220	
221	   - **On schedule**: elapsed ≤ estimate.
222	   - **Slipping**: elapsed > estimate but ≤ 2× estimate.
223	   - **Over budget**: elapsed > 2× estimate.
224	   - **Estimate-free**: STRATEGY.md gives no number, or "?", for this row.
225	
226	   "Over budget" with a still-positive `Iters left` is the dishonest-estimate signature. "Estimate-free" with elapsed > 5 iters in a phase is also flag-worthy.
227	
228	## Verdict rules
229	
230	Apply these rules verbatim:
231	
232	- **CONVERGING**: sorry count strictly decreasing in K-iter window AND no recurring blocker AND no under-dispatch pattern AND no avoidance pattern AND planner's proposal looks like "finish what's started."
233	- **CHURNING**: any of the following:
234	  - helpers added in ≥2 of last K iters AND sorry count net unchanged or down by <1 per 2 iters AND no structural change in approach;
235	  - PARTIAL prover status ≥3 of last K iters;
236	  - **plan-phase-only meta-pattern**: ≥3 consecutive iters with zero prover dispatches on this route (no file ever appearing in `## Current Objectives`). Pure planning rounds without ever firing a prover is textbook stall.
237	  - **under-dispatch pattern**: prover count = 1 (or otherwise N < M ready) for ≥2 consecutive iters with no sorry-trajectory improvement attributable to the dispatch strategy (i.e. the filed sorry on the dispatched lane didn't close either).
238	  - **avoidance pattern**: ≥2 consecutive iters with "off-critical path" / "deferred" route status AND no re-engagement plan in the proposal.
239	- **STUCK**: sorry count unchanged across K iters AND prover statuses include INCOMPLETE OR recurring blocker phrase across ≥3 iters. OR: helpers added without any sorry-elimination across K iters. OR: same deferral phrase persisting across ≥2 consecutive iters.
240	- **UNCLEAR**: route is fresh (< K iters of data) OR signals are ambiguous.
241	
242	If multiple rules match a route, pick the worse verdict (CHURNING > CONVERGING; STUCK > CHURNING).
243	
244	## Recommended actions per verdict
245	
246	For CHURNING or STUCK, your report names ONE primary corrective TYPE. The planner consults the catalog for the matching subagent.
247	
248	- **Blueprint expansion** — the chapter's proof sketch is likely under-specified; the planner should expand it before more prover work.
249	- **Mathlib analogy consult** — the project may be using a parallel API or wrong predicate; the planner should consult Mathlib-idiom analysis on the route's load-bearing definitions.
250	- **Refactor** — the definition or file structure may be wrong; dispatch a structural subagent before more prover work.
251	- **Route pivot** — the strategic route may be wrong entirely; revise STRATEGY.md and pick a different route, then re-run any strategy critic mid-iter to validate.
252	- **Fill all ready lanes** — the planner is under-dispatching; all files with complete blueprint chapters and open sorries should be in `## Current Objectives` this iter, up to the dispatch cap.
253	- **Address deferred infrastructure** — the route has been marked off-critical-path or deferred; the planner must either write the blueprint chapter and open a prover lane this iter, or explicitly close the route as out-of-scope (which requires updating `## Goal` in STRATEGY.md if the goal depends on it).
254	- **User escalation** — none of the above will work; pause and request user input. Use sparingly — only when no automated corrective will resolve the stall.
255	
256	Pick ONE primary corrective per CHURNING/STUCK route. Multiple are allowed when truly necessary, listed in priority order.
257	
258	## Report format
259	
260	Write your report to `.archon/task_results/progress-critic-<slug>.md`.
261	
262	**CRITICAL COST RULE**: Your report must be extremely concise to save LLM tokens. Use dense bullet points, abbreviations, and zero conversational filler. DO NOT write paragraphs. Omit empty sections. The plan agent only needs facts.
263	
264	```markdown
265	# Progress Critic: <slug>
266	**Iter:** <NNN>
267	
268	## Routes
269	<For each route, max 2-3 lines:>
270	- **`Foo.lean`**: <CONVERGING|CHURNING|STUCK|UNCLEAR>. Sorry 1->1, +9 helpers.
271	  - Corrective: <Name action, e.g. "mathlib-build L1 bridge">
272	
273	## Dispatch Sanity
274	- **Verdict**: <OK | OVER_CAP | UNDER_DISPATCH | BLOAT_WITHOUT_PROGRESS>. <1 line context>.
275	
276	## Must-fix-this-iter
277	<OMIT if empty. Max 1 line per item.>
278	- Route `Foo.lean`: CHURNING - needs L1 bridge.
279	- Dispatch: UNDER_DISPATCH.
280	
281	## Overall
282	- <1 sentence summary: e.g., "1 converging, 1 stuck (needs L1 bridge), dispatch OK.">
283	```
284	
285	## Return value
286	
287	Your final assistant message:
288	
289	- One line: `<slug>: <overall verdict> — <N> routes audited, <M> CHURNING/STUCK verdicts, <K> avoidance findings, dispatch=<OK|OVER_CAP|UNDER_DISPATCH|BLOAT_WITHOUT_PROGRESS>`
290	- The path to your full report.
291	
292	## Reminders
293	
294	- **You don't read strategy or blueprint.** Convergence is the question; soundness is for other subagents.
295	- **No bias toward CONVERGING.** The planner wants the route to be CONVERGING; you do not. Apply the verdict rules verbatim.
296	- **Under-dispatch is churn.** Sending one prover when three files are ready burns iterations just as surely as adding helpers that don't close sorries. Treat it as a planning failure, not a scheduling preference.
297	- **Avoidance is churn.** "Off-critical path" reclassification, consecutive plan-only iters, and persistent deferral language are all CHURNING signals. They indicate the planner is iterating around the hard problem instead of through it.
298	- **One primary corrective per route.** Don't list five and let the planner pick.
299	- **Recurring blockers are signal, not noise.** When the same blocker phrase appears across 3+ iters, the route is stuck regardless of helper counts.

1	# Archon Project
2	
3	You are one of: the plan agent, a prover agent, a subagent (per descriptor in `.archon/subagents/`), or the review agent. Read `PROGRESS.md` to determine your role and current objectives. Keep workspace tidy. Prefer existing MCP tools.
4	
5	## Priority Rule
6	
7	When instructions conflict between global and local sources, **local takes precedence**:
8	
9	- Prompts in `.archon/prompts/` override Archon's global prompts.
10	- Under Claude Code, skills in `.claude/skills/` override globally installed plugins.
11	- Under Claude Code, rules in `.claude/rules/` apply only to this project.
12	
13	## Skills
14	
15	- **archon-lean4** — under Claude Code this is installed as the `lean4@archon-local` plugin (live-linked to Archon source), providing slash commands `/archon-lean4:prove`, `/archon-lean4:golf`, `/archon-lean4:doctor`, etc. Under other harnesses the slash-command surface is absent; the underlying skill scripts are still on disk under `.claude/skills/` and can be run directly from the shell.
16	
17	## Tools
18	
19	Project tools live in `.claude/tools/` as directly-executable scripts — that path is the same whatever harness you run under. List with `ls .claude/tools/`; run `<path> --help`. MCP servers (Lean LSP, etc.) surface as `mcp__*` tools: under Claude Code they are registered in `.claude/settings.json` / `.mcp.json`; under other harnesses the loop wires the same servers in at launch.
20	
21	Two always-present scripts:
22	
23	- **`archon-informal-agent.py`** — call external LLMs for an informal proof-style sketch when you're stuck on an approach and want a second opinion. Supports DeepSeek, Kimi (OpenAI-compatible via `--provider kimi`), Kimi (Anthropic-compatible via `--provider kimi-anthropic`), OpenRouter, OpenAI, and Gemini; defaults to `--provider auto` which picks the best available key automatically. **Requires at least one key in env** (`DEEPSEEK_API_KEY` / `MOONSHOT_API_KEY` / `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` / `OPENAI_API_KEY` / `GEMINI_API_KEY`) — check via `env | grep -E "DEEPSEEK|MOONSHOT|OPENROUTER|OPENAI|GEMINI"` BEFORE planning around its use; if no key is set, fall back to alternatives below. Both `kimi` and `kimi-anthropic` use the same `MOONSHOT_API_KEY`; the latter speaks Moonshot's Anthropic-compatible Messages endpoint and supports `--think`. The output is LLM-generated from training data, NOT source-derived: this tool is **not** a literature retriever. For actual literature lookup consult the auto-injected subagent catalog — when a literature/reference-fetching subagent is enabled it will appear there — or use the `WebSearch` / `WebFetch` tools directly.
24	- **`archon-subagent.py`** — the generic subagent dispatcher (one wrapper handles every subagent — see "Subagents" below).
25	
26	## Subagents
27	
28	Subagents are descriptor files at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` (YAML frontmatter — `name`, `description`, `write_domain`, `read_only`, `can_spawn`, `default_enabled`, optional `mandatory: [<phase>...]`, optional `dispatcher_notes` — followed by the prompt body the spawned agent reads).
29	
30	**You do NOT need to discover subagents.** The plan and review prompts auto-inject an **Available subagents** section at the top of each invocation listing every enabled descriptor with description, write-domain, MANDATORY flag, and `dispatcher_notes`. When you decide to invoke a subagent, read its full prompt at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md`.
31	
32	**Invoke** via the generic wrapper (Bash tool, foreground):
33	
34	```
35	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
36	  --name <subagent-name> \
37	  --slug <kebab-slug> \
38	  --directive-file <path-to-directive.md> \
39	  --write-domain '<glob>'        # repeat for multiple
40	```
41	
42	The wrapper exits 0 on success, prints a one-line status, writes the report to `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`, and the Archon CLI auto-archives a copy to `logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-report.md` for the dashboard. The dispatch semaphore caps concurrent subagent processes at `loop.max_parallel`; the wrapper resolves `--parent-slug` from `ARCHON_SUBAGENT_SLUG` so nested children inherit the hierarchy.
43	
44	**A dispatch is a BLOCKING Bash call — wait by staying inside it.** `archon-subagent.py` is synchronous (it `subprocess.run`s the `archon subagent` CLI and returns only once the child finishes and its report is on disk). A long dispatch may be auto-backgrounded with a task ID — that's fine; wait for that task's result before acting. **Do NOT use the `Monitor` tool to wait** — it is non-blocking (returns immediately, "keep working"), so your turn ends and the subagents are abandoned mid-run. **Do NOT use foreground `sleep` / `until … ; do sleep … ; done`** — the harness blocks `sleep`. To run independent subagents **in parallel**, put all their dispatches in **one Bash call**, each backgrounded with `&`, ending with `wait` — e.g. `python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py --name A … & python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py --name B … & wait` — which blocks until all finish (the whole wave as one blocking dispatch). Serialize only subagents that write the **same file** (separate sequential calls). The rule never changes: do not consume a subagent's output until its report exists.
45	
46	**Highly-recommended subagents.** A descriptor whose frontmatter sets `mandatory: [plan]` is treated as **highly recommended** for the plan phase (similarly `[review]` for the review phase). The catalog tags them `[HIGHLY RECOMMENDED]`. Dispatch each one unless you have a concrete reason to skip — when skipping, record a one-line rationale under a `## Subagent skips` section in `iter/iter-NNN/<phase>.md` (e.g. `- strategy-critic: STRATEGY.md SHA unchanged from prior iter and last verdict was SOUND`). A post-phase audit checks both signals: it silences when the subagent dispatched OR a skip rationale was recorded, and warns only when neither happened. Each subagent's `dispatcher_notes` enumerates its specific skip conditions — read them before deciding. **Subagents ship disabled by default** (`default_enabled: false`), so the rule only fires for subagents the user has explicitly listed under `subagents.enabled` in `.archon/config.json`; on a fresh project the catalog is empty and no recommended dispatch is required.
47	
48	## Key Files & Permissions
49	
50	All Archon state files are in `.archon/`. The table below covers **phase agents** (plan, prover, review) and the **user**. Subagent permissions are NOT a fixed row — they are descriptor-driven (`write_domain` glob in the subagent's frontmatter, enforced at dispatch time). See "Subagent permissions" after the table.
51	
52	| File | Plan | Prover | Review | User |
53	|------|------|--------|--------|------|
54	| `PROGRESS.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
55	| `STRATEGY.md` | read + write | — | — | read |
56	| `ARCHON_MEMORY.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read (edits via `archon discuss`) |
57	| `USER_HINTS.md` | **loop-managed** (captured + cleared by the CLI; injected into the plan prompt) | — | — | write |
58	| `task_pending.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
59	| `task_done.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
60	| `task_results/<file>.md` | read (collect) | write (own file) | read only | read |
61	| `proof-journal/` | read | — | **write** | read |
62	| `PROJECT_STATUS.md` | read | — | **write** (Knowledge Base only — session log lives in `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`) | read |
63	| `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md` | **write** (this iter only) | — | read (recent window injected) | read |
64	| `iter/iter-NNN/review.md` | read (recent window injected) | — | **write** (this iter only) | read |
65	| `iter/iter-NNN/objectives.md` | optional write | — | read | read |
66	| `archon-protected.yaml` | read | read | read | **write** |
67	| `.lean` files | — | write (own file; frozen protected signatures) | — | write (via comments) |
68	| `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex` | **write** (informal prose, `\lean{...}` hints) | read | **write** (semantic markers: `\mathlibok`, `% NOTE:`, `\lean{...}` corrections — NOT `\leanok`) | read |
69	| `analogies/<slug>.md` | read (when relevant) | — | — | read |
70	| `TO_USER.md` | **write** (shared notice board) | **write** (shared notice board) | **write** (shared notice board) | read |
71	
72	### Subagent permissions
73	
74	Each subagent ships YAML frontmatter:
75	
76	- `write_domain: "<glob>"` — file pattern the subagent may write. Empty for purely read-only subagents (the dispatching call typically passes `--write-domain 'task_results/**'` so the subagent can still write its own report).
77	- `read_only: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may only write its `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`.
78	- `can_spawn: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may dispatch children whose `write_domain` is a subset of its own.
79	
80	The dispatcher enforces `write_domain` at the filesystem level: any child whose declared domain is not a subset of the parent's is rejected before the child agent starts. Reads are restricted only by the descriptor's prompt body (e.g. fresh-context critics that are told not to read PROGRESS.md / STRATEGY.md).
81	
82	**`.archon/REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md`** is reserved for the interactive `archon refactor draft` / `archon refactor run` flow run by hand by the mathematician. The autonomous loop never reads or writes that file. To invoke a refactor-style subagent inside the loop, write the directive to a fresh tempfile under `.archon/logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-directive.md` and dispatch via the generic wrapper.
83	
84	## Protected Declarations
85	
86	`archon-protected.yaml` at the project root lists declarations whose **signatures are frozen** by the mathematician. Rules:
87	
88	- **Plan / prover / review** agents: read-only on protected signatures. May fill proof bodies; may not rename, re-type, or reorder arguments.
89	- **Write-capable structural subagents** (per their `write_domain`): may *move* a protected declaration to a different file (name + signature preserved verbatim) and must then update the path key in `archon-protected.yaml`. They may NEVER rename / re-type / delete / re-sign a protected declaration.
90	- The YAML file itself is user-only. No agent adds or removes a declaration. The only allowed agent edit is updating an existing declaration's file path.
91	
92	## Blueprint Marker Vocabulary
93	
94	Two semantic markers:
95	
96	- `\leanok` — statement block: declaration is formalized (at least a `sorry` present). Proof block: proof closed, no `sorry`.
97	- `\mathlibok` — statement block: declaration is backed by Mathlib (re-export / alias); no Archon proof obligation remains.
98	
99	No marker means the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.
100	
101	**`\leanok` is managed deterministically by the `sync_leanok` phase** that runs between prover and review each iteration. It walks every chapter, looks up each `\lean{...}` declaration, runs `sorry_analyzer` + `lake build <module>` (it uses `lake build`, not `lake env lean <file>`, so dependencies are built and missing-import failures aren't spurious), and adds/removes `\leanok` accordingly. Agents should NOT add or remove `\leanok` as routine bookkeeping — leave it to the sync. The one exception is the review agent: if it is **certain** the sync got a marker wrong (a clearly-finished proof left unmarked, or a marker present on something incomplete), it may manually correct it, but must explicitly surface and justify that override in its summary (see `.archon/prompts/review.md`).
102	
103	`\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, and `% NOTE:` annotations are the review agent's domain (they require semantic judgement). The plan agent writes informal prose and `\lean{...}` hints; provers never touch the blueprint.
104	
105	## User Interaction
106	
107	The user provides hints in two places:
108	
109	- **Strategic hints** → `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The Archon loop captures this file's content before each plan phase, injects it into the plan agent's prompt, and resets the file to its bundled template (an HTML-comment format guide + zero bullets) after the plan phase succeeds. The HTML-comment preamble is stripped before injection so a template-only file renders as "no hints" to the planner. The plan agent does NOT read or clear that file itself. Provers never see these hints.
110	- **File-specific hints** → `/- USER: ... -/` comments directly in `.lean` files. The prover that owns that file sees them naturally.
111	
112	Archon talks back to the user through **`TO_USER.md`** — a single, *persistent* notice board surfaced as a UI banner. It is **shared and writable by every agent** (plan, prover, review). It is no longer cleared at the start of each review phase; content survives across iters until an agent prunes it. The discipline every writer follows:
113	
114	- **Concise**: the whole file stays ≤ 2–3 short bullets. It is a banner, not a log.
115	- **Relevant now**: before adding anything, read the current content and **delete any bullet that is no longer true or important at this point** (the blocker was resolved, the decision was overtaken, the credential got set). Never let it accumulate stale notices.
116	- **Notice board, never a question queue**: the loop is autonomous and may run unattended for many iters. Never write "awaiting your decision", an options menu, a "where to reply", or "default to X if no reply" — anything implying the loop is paused for a human. State decisions as *made* and blockers as *worked-around-as-far-as-possible*; the user steers asynchronously via `USER_HINTS.md`.
117	
118	## Agent Roles
119	
120	### Plan Agent
121	
122	- Read `.archon/prompts/plan.md` for full instructions.
123	- User hints, blueprint-doctor findings, recent iter sidecars, the subagent catalog, and the references summary are all pre-injected into your invocation prompt. You do not "go read" those files.
124	- Read `task_results/` to collect prover and subagent results, then update `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md`.
125	- Optionally invoke subagents (via the auto-injected catalog) before writing prover objectives. Subagent reports are auto-archived to `logs/iter-NNN/` by the Archon CLI.
126	- Write `PROGRESS.md` with objectives for the next prover round.
127	- Curate `ARCHON_MEMORY.md` — the condensed, cross-iteration knowledge file injected (read-only) into every agent's prompt. Keep only durable facts that would surprise an agent reading the code fresh (dead-end tactics, files not to touch, Mathlib gap coordinates, protected invariants). Hard limits: ~10 bullets / ~600 chars; prune before adding. Do NOT duplicate what's obvious from the code or PROGRESS.md.
128	- Write informal prose in `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`. Do NOT touch markers — `\leanok` is owned by the deterministic sync; `\mathlibok` / `% NOTE:` by the review agent.
129	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or fill sorries yourself.
130	
131	### Prover Agent
132	
133	- Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
134	- Your **active mode** is injected directly into your invocation prompt — read it carefully, it defines your goal, workflow, and constraints for this session.
135	- When your mode specifies `read_blueprint: true`, you are permitted to **read** your assigned blueprint chapter (`blueprint/src/chapters/<your_slug>.tex`) for the informal proof sketch. You may never write to blueprint chapters.
136	- Write results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.
137	- A prover lane ends by writing its task result and exiting normally. **Do not kill Lean, Lake, shell, or sibling agent processes from inside the prover lane.** Do not run `pkill`, `killall`, regex-based process cleanup, or background-verification cleanup. If a verification command is still running, wait for its result or report that it is still running; process lifetime is owned by the Archon runner/orchestrator.
138	- Write only to the `.lean` file(s) you are assigned — never edit another agent's file.
139	- Check for `/- USER: ... -/` comments in your file for file-specific hints.
140	- Do NOT edit blueprint chapters. Flag in your task result which declarations are ready for which marker; the review agent applies them.
141	
142	### Subagents
143	
144	Subagents are descriptor-driven and discovered at runtime. The catalog is auto-injected into the plan and review prompts each phase (see "Subagents" above for the wrapper invocation). Each subagent reads only what its directive points at — never `PROGRESS.md` / `STRATEGY.md` / phase-agent state — and writes a self-contained report at `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`. Descriptors with `can_spawn: true` may dispatch children (subject to the global `loop.max_parallel` cap).
145	
146	### Review Agent
147	
148	- Read `.archon/prompts/review.md` for full instructions.
149	- Read `proof-journal/current_session/attempts_raw.jsonl` for structured prover attempt data.
150	- Write the session journal to `proof-journal/sessions/session_N/` (`summary.md`, `milestones.jsonl`, `recommendations.md`).
151	- Update `PROJECT_STATUS.md` (Knowledge Base only — session narrative goes to `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`).
152	- Maintain the semantic blueprint markers — `\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, `% NOTE:` annotations, stale `\notready` removal. Normally leave `\leanok` to the deterministic `sync_leanok` phase; only override a marker the sync got demonstrably wrong, and justify it in your summary.
153	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or modify `PROGRESS.md`.
154	
155	### Loop infrastructure (no agent role)
156	
157	These run automatically between phases:
158	
159	- **Iter sidecar init** — creates `.archon/iter/iter-NNN/` at iter start so plan + review have a stable destination for per-iter narrative. Top-level files (STRATEGY.md, PROJECT_STATUS.md, task_*.md) stay bounded.
160	- **`sync_leanok`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically updating `\leanok` markers based on actual sorry counts and compilation status.
161	- **`blueprint-doctor`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically detecting orphan chapters, broken `\ref` / `\uses` / `\proves`, and new `axiom` declarations. Findings are written to `logs/iter-NNN/blueprint-doctor.{md,json}` and inlined into the next plan agent's prompt.
162

6	import Mathlib
7	import AlgebraicJacobian.Cohomology.CechAugmentedResolution
8	import AlgebraicJacobian.Cohomology.OpenImmersionPushforward
9	import AlgebraicJacobian.Cohomology.AcyclicResolution
10	import AlgebraicJacobian.Cohomology.CechTermAcyclic
11	
12	/-!
13	# Čech computation of higher direct images — capstone leaf (correct hypotheses)
14	
15	This file is the downstream leaf hosting the Route-A capstone: the Čech-to-derived-pushforward
16	comparison `cech_computes_higherDirectImage_of_affineCover` under the **correct** hypotheses
17	`[X.IsSeparated]` and `h𝒰 : ∀ i, IsAffine (𝒰.X i)`.
18	
19	The companion `CechHigherDirectImage.lean` contains the frozen declaration
20	`cech_computes_higherDirectImage`, which omits those two hypotheses and is left as a `sorry`
21	(escalated to the mathematician).  The present file introduces the correctly-stated variant
22	that the prover can close.
23	
24	Blueprint chapter: `blueprint/src/chapters/Cohomology_CechHigherDirectImage.tex`,
25	blocks at lines L11819 (`lem:rightAcyclic_finite_prod`), L11635 (`lem:cech_term_pushforward_acyclic`),
26	L11845 (`lem:pushforward_mapHC_cechComplexOnX`), L11885 (`lem:cechAugmented_to_acyclicResolutionInput`),
27	L11926 (`lem:cech_computes_cohomology_affineCover`).
28	-/
29	
30	universe u
31	
32	open CategoryTheory Limits
33	
34	namespace AlgebraicGeometry
35	
36	open Scheme.Modules
37	
38	variable {X S : Scheme.{u}}
39	
40	/-! ## Pushforward commutes with the Čech complex functor -/
41	
42	/- Planner strategy: lem:pushforward_mapHC_cechComplexOnX ·
43	Both complexes are built as `alternatingCofaceMapComplex` of the same cosimplicial object, differing
44	only in whether `f_*` is applied before or after the alternating coface construction.  Since `f_*`
45	is additive, `(f_*).mapHomologicalComplex` commutes with `alternatingCofaceMapComplex` by the
46	naturality of `CosimplicialObject.whiskering` and the fact that the alternating-coface differential
47	is an alternating sum that `f_*` preserves by additivity.
48	Concretely: `cechComplexOnX 𝒰 F = alternatingCofaceMapComplex.obj (drop (CechNerve 𝒰 F))`,
49	and `CechComplex f 𝒰 F = relativeCechComplexOfNerve f (CechNerve 𝒰 F)
50	                        = alternatingCofaceMapComplex.obj (f_* ∘ drop (CechNerve 𝒰 F))`.
51	The iso is the natural isomorphism between the two functors
52	`alternatingCofaceMapComplex ∘ whiskering(f_*)` and `(f_*).mapHomologicalComplex ∘ alternatingCofaceMapComplex`
53	applied to the same cosimplicial object; the components are identities in each degree. -/
54	/-- **An additive functor commutes with the alternating coface map complex** (object-level
55	cosimplicial analogue of `AlgebraicTopology.map_alternatingFaceMapComplex`). The components are
56	identities: in each degree both complexes have the object `G.obj (Y.obj ⦋p⦌)`, and the
57	differential of the whiskered complex is `G` applied to the alternating coface differential,
58	by additivity (`Functor.map_sum`, `Functor.map_zsmul`). Project-local helper. -/
59	noncomputable def mapAlternatingCofaceMapComplexIso
60	    {C D : Type*} [Category C] [Category D] [Preadditive C] [Preadditive D]
61	    (G : C ⥤ D) [G.Additive] (Y : CosimplicialObject C) :
62	    (G.mapHomologicalComplex (ComplexShape.up ℕ)).obj
63	        ((AlgebraicTopology.alternatingCofaceMapComplex C).obj Y) ≅
64	      (AlgebraicTopology.alternatingCofaceMapComplex D).obj
65	        (((CosimplicialObject.whiskering C D).obj G).obj Y) :=
66	  HomologicalComplex.Hom.isoOfComponents (fun _ => Iso.refl _) (by
67	    rintro i j (rfl : i + 1 = j)
68	    simp only [Iso.refl_hom, Category.id_comp, Category.comp_id,
69	      Functor.mapHomologicalComplex_obj_d, AlgebraicTopology.alternatingCofaceMapComplex_obj]
70	    dsimp only [AlgebraicTopology.AlternatingCofaceMapComplex.obj]
71	    rw [CochainComplex.of_d, CochainComplex.of_d]
72	    simp only [AlgebraicTopology.AlternatingCofaceMapComplex.objD, Functor.map_sum,
73	      Functor.map_zsmul]
74	    rfl)
75	
76	/-- **The `f_*`-image of the un-augmented Čech complex on `X` is isomorphic to the relative Čech
77	complex** (blueprint `lem:pushforward_mapHC_cechComplexOnX`). -/
78	noncomputable def pushforward_mapHomologicalComplex_cechComplexOnX
79	    (f : X ⟶ S) (𝒰 : X.OpenCover) (F : X.Modules) :
80	    ((Scheme.Modules.pushforward f).mapHomologicalComplex (ComplexShape.up ℕ)).obj
81	        (cechComplexOnX 𝒰 F) ≅ CechComplex f 𝒰 F :=
82	  -- `cechComplexOnX` and `CechComplex` are *definitionally* the alternating coface complexes of
83	  -- the (un-whiskered, resp. `f_*`-whiskered) underlying cosimplicial object of the Čech nerve,
84	  -- so the general helper applies on the nose.
85	  mapAlternatingCofaceMapComplexIso (Scheme.Modules.pushforward f)
86	    (CosimplicialObject.Augmented.drop.obj (CechNerve 𝒰 F))
87	
88	/-! ## From augmented exactness to the acyclic-resolution input data -/
89	
90	/- Planner strategy: lem:cechAugmented_to_acyclicResolutionInput ·
91	From `cechAugmented_exact` (CechAugmentedResolution.lean) we have:
92	  `∀ p, IsZero ((cechAugmentedComplex 𝒰 F).homology p)`.
93	The augmented complex has `X 0 = F` and `X (n+1) = (cechComplexOnX 𝒰 F).X n`; its differential at
94	degree 0 is the augmentation `ε : F → C⁰`.
95	
96	(1) Exactness of `cechComplexOnX 𝒰 F` at degree `n+1`:  the augmented complex at degree `n+2`
97	    coincides with the un-augmented complex at degree `n+1`.  Use
98	    `HomologicalComplex.exactAt_iff_isZero_homology` plus the vanishing from `cechAugmented_exact`.
99	
100	(2) Iso `e : F ≅ (cechComplexOnX 𝒰 F).cycles 0`:  vanishing of homology at degree 0 gives that
101	    ε is a monomorphism; vanishing at degree 1 gives that the image of ε equals `ker d⁰ = cycles 0`.
102	    Hence ε is an iso onto `cycles 0`.  The iso is assembled from the augmentation `cechAugmentation`
103	    and the exactness data; use `ShortComplex.Exact.isoOfEpiMonoIsZero` or similar.
104	
105	Both outputs are assembled into a `PProd` (anonymous constructor `⟨e, hexact⟩`; `PProd` rather
106	than `Prod` because the second component is a `Prop` while the first is an `Iso` in `Type`). -/
107	/-- **From augmented exactness to the P4 input data**
108	(blueprint `lem:cechAugmented_to_acyclicResolutionInput`).
109	
110	Given the hypotheses of `cechAugmented_exact`, this declaration packages the two pieces of data
111	that `rightDerivedIsoOfAcyclicResolution` (the abstract acyclic-resolution lemma) requires:
112	an isomorphism `e : F ≅ (cechComplexOnX 𝒰 F).cycles 0` identifying `F` with the 0-cocycles,
113	and exactness `(cechComplexOnX 𝒰 F).ExactAt (n+1)` in every positive degree. -/
114	noncomputable def cechAugmented_to_acyclicResolutionInput
115	    (𝒰 : X.OpenCover) [Finite 𝒰.I₀] (h𝒰 : ∀ i, IsAffine (𝒰.X i)) [X.IsSeparated]
116	    (F : X.Modules) (hF : F.IsQuasicoherent) :
117	    (F ≅ (cechComplexOnX 𝒰 F).cycles 0) ×' (∀ n, (cechComplexOnX 𝒰 F).ExactAt (n + 1)) := by
118	  have hKex : ∀ p, (cechAugmentedComplex 𝒰 F).ExactAt p := fun p =>
119	    (HomologicalComplex.exactAt_iff_isZero_homology _ p).2 (cechAugmented_exact 𝒰 h𝒰 F hF p)
120	  -- (2) positive-degree exactness: the short complex of the augmented complex around `n+2`
121	  -- is definitionally the short complex of the un-augmented complex around `n+1`.
122	  have hexact : ∀ n, (cechComplexOnX 𝒰 F).ExactAt (n + 1) := by
123	    intro n
124	    have h := hKex (n + 2)
125	    rw [(cechAugmentedComplex 𝒰 F).exactAt_iff' (n + 1) (n + 2) (n + 3)
126	      ((ComplexShape.up ℕ).prev_eq' rfl) ((ComplexShape.up ℕ).next_eq' rfl)] at h
127	    rw [(cechComplexOnX 𝒰 F).exactAt_iff' n (n + 1) (n + 2)
128	      ((ComplexShape.up ℕ).prev_eq' rfl) ((ComplexShape.up ℕ).next_eq' rfl)]
129	    exact h
130	  -- ε is a monomorphism: the augmented complex is exact at 0 and its incoming map vanishes.
131	  have h0 := hKex 0
132	  rw [(cechAugmentedComplex 𝒰 F).exactAt_iff' 0 0 1 CochainComplex.prev_nat_zero
133	    ((ComplexShape.up ℕ).next_eq' rfl)] at h0
134	  haveI hmono : Mono (cechAugmentation 𝒰 F) :=
135	    h0.mono_g ((cechAugmentedComplex 𝒰 F).shape 0 0 (by simp))
136	  -- exactness of the augmented complex at 1: the short complex `F –ε→ C⁰ –d⁰→ C¹`.
137	  have h1 := hKex 1
138	  rw [(cechAugmentedComplex 𝒰 F).exactAt_iff' 0 1 2
139	    ((ComplexShape.up ℕ).prev_eq' rfl) ((ComplexShape.up ℕ).next_eq' rfl)] at h1
140	  haveI : Mono ((cechAugmentedComplex 𝒰 F).sc' 0 1 2).f := hmono
141	  -- `ι' ≫ ε = iCycles`: the exactness-lift of the cycle inclusion through ε.
142	  have hl : h1.lift ((cechComplexOnX 𝒰 F).iCycles 0) ((cechComplexOnX 𝒰 F).iCycles_d 0 1) ≫
143	      cechAugmentation 𝒰 F = (cechComplexOnX 𝒰 F).iCycles 0 := h1.lift_f _ _
144	  refine ⟨⟨(cechComplexOnX 𝒰 F).liftCycles (cechAugmentation 𝒰 F) 1
145	      ((ComplexShape.up ℕ).next_eq' rfl) (cechAugmentation_comp_d 𝒰 F),
146	    h1.lift ((cechComplexOnX 𝒰 F).iCycles 0) ((cechComplexOnX 𝒰 F).iCycles_d 0 1),
147	    ?_, ?_⟩, hexact⟩
148	  · -- hom_inv_id: cancel the monomorphism ε on the right.
149	    rw [← cancel_mono (cechAugmentation 𝒰 F), Category.assoc, Category.id_comp, hl,
150	      HomologicalComplex.liftCycles_i]
151	  · -- inv_hom_id: cancel the monomorphism `iCycles` on the right.
152	    rw [← cancel_mono ((cechComplexOnX 𝒰 F).iCycles 0), Category.assoc, Category.id_comp,
153	      HomologicalComplex.liftCycles_i, hl]
154	
155	/-! ## Capstone: Čech computes higher direct images (affine-cover form) -/
156	
157	/- Planner strategy: lem:cech_computes_cohomology_affineCover ·
158	Assembly of the four Route-A ingredients:
159	
160	(a) `cechAugmented_to_acyclicResolutionInput` yields:
161	    · `e : F ≅ (cechComplexOnX 𝒰 F).cycles 0`
162	    · `hexact : ∀ n, (cechComplexOnX 𝒰 F).ExactAt (n+1)`
163	
164	(b) `cechTerm_pushforward_acyclic` provides the typeclass instance:
165	    `[∀ p, (Scheme.Modules.pushforward f).IsRightAcyclic ((cechComplexOnX 𝒰 F).X p)]`
166	    (introduce with `haveI` for each `p`; or use `inferInstance` if the `∀ p` form is
167	    synthesisable from a blanket instance).
168	
169	(c) `Functor.rightDerivedIsoOfAcyclicResolution` (AcyclicResolution.lean, fully proved) with
170	    G = `Scheme.Modules.pushforward f`, K = `cechComplexOnX 𝒰 F`, A = F, gives:
171	    `((Scheme.Modules.pushforward f).rightDerived i).obj F
172	      ≅ ((G.mapHomologicalComplex (ComplexShape.up ℕ)).obj (cechComplexOnX 𝒰 F)).homology i`
173	
174	(d) `pushforward_mapHomologicalComplex_cechComplexOnX` rewrites the right-hand side to
175	    `(CechComplex f 𝒰 F).homology i`.
176	
177	The final iso `(CechComplex f 𝒰 F).homology i ≅ higherDirectImage f i F` is the composite of
178	(d).symm, (c).symm, noting `higherDirectImage f i F = ((pushforward f).rightDerived i).obj F`.
179	Wrap in `Nonempty` via `⟨iso⟩`.
180	
181	Additive / PreservesFiniteLimits hypotheses on `pushforward f`: `Additive` is an instance;
182	`PreservesFiniteLimits` is needed for `rightDerivedIsoOfAcyclicResolution` (via
183	`PreservesFiniteLimits (Scheme.Modules.pushforward f)` — left-exact since it is a right adjoint
184	via the global sections adjunction). -/
185	/-- **Čech computes higher direct images — affine-cover form**
186	(blueprint `lem:cech_computes_cohomology_affineCover`; Stacks Tag 02KE).
187	
188	Let `f : X ⟶ S` be a separated quasi-compact morphism with `X` separated, `F` a quasi-coherent
189	`O_X`-module, and `𝒰` a finite affine open cover of `X`.  Then for every `i ≥ 0` there is an
190	isomorphism between the `i`-th cohomology of the relative Čech complex and the `i`-th higher
191	direct image:
192	```
193	  (CechComplex f 𝒰 F).homology i ≅ R^i f_* F  =  higherDirectImage f i F.
194	```
195	This is the correctly-stated capstone; the frozen `cech_computes_higherDirectImage` in
196	`CechHigherDirectImage.lean` omits `[X.IsSeparated]` and `h𝒰` and is escalated. -/
197	theorem cech_computes_higherDirectImage_of_affineCover [HasInjectiveResolutions X.Modules]
198	    (f : X ⟶ S) [QuasiCompact f] [IsSeparated f] [X.IsSeparated]
199	    (𝒰 : X.OpenCover) [Finite 𝒰.I₀] (h𝒰 : ∀ i, IsAffine (𝒰.X i))
200	    (F : X.Modules) (hF : F.IsQuasicoherent) (i : ℕ) :
201	    Nonempty ((CechComplex f 𝒰 F).homology i ≅ higherDirectImage f i F) := by
202	  -- (a) the resolution data extracted from the exactness of the augmented Čech complex
203	  obtain ⟨e, hexact⟩ := cechAugmented_to_acyclicResolutionInput 𝒰 h𝒰 F hF
204	  -- (b) termwise right-`f_*`-acyclicity of the Čech complex on `X` (black box from
205	  -- `CechTermAcyclic.lean`)
206	  haveI : ∀ n, (Scheme.Modules.pushforward f).IsRightAcyclic ((cechComplexOnX 𝒰 F).X n) :=
207	    fun n => cechTerm_pushforward_acyclic f 𝒰 h𝒰 F hF n
208	  -- (c) `f_*` is left exact: it is a right adjoint (of the module pullback)
209	  haveI : PreservesLimits (Scheme.Modules.pushforward f) :=
210	    (Scheme.Modules.pullbackPushforwardAdjunction f).rightAdjoint_preservesLimits
211	  -- (d) assemble: P4 acyclic-resolution comparison, then rewrite `f_* C•` to the relative
212	  -- Čech complex; `higherDirectImage f i F` is definitionally `((f_*).rightDerived i).obj F`.
213	  exact ⟨(HomologicalComplex.homologyFunctor S.Modules (ComplexShape.up ℕ) i).mapIso
214	      (pushforward_mapHomologicalComplex_cechComplexOnX f 𝒰 F).symm ≪≫
215	    ((Scheme.Modules.pushforward f).rightDerivedIsoOfAcyclicResolution
216	      (cechComplexOnX 𝒰 F) F e hexact i).symm⟩
217	
218	end AlgebraicGeometry

- Phase P5b has been active since iter-067 (~11 elapsed), but the capstone was not the active leaf for most of those iters (CSI + CechAugmented were the active leaves). No throughput inflation signal specific to the capstone.
